Server claims it has tools but doesn't validate → attacker exploits

of what the server requests (like browser camera prompts)
revealing arm cards at a poker table

Player A: "I have Aces and Kings"  ← Client declares capabilities
Player B: "I have Queens and Jacks" ← Server declares capabilities
Both: "Okay, now we play"          ← initialized notification

After the reveal:
- Player A can't suddenly play a "Flush" (not declared)
- Player B can't demand Player A draw cards (sampling not declared)
- Both know exactly what moves are valid
New capabilities added through protocol extensions
: Client declares what it supports; server declares what it provides
: Defines trust boundaries for the entire session
: Neither side can use undeclared capabilities
: Both sides agree on protocol version
: Only methods explicitly advertised can be used
Capability negotiation is MCP's way of ensuring
on what a session can do—preventing both security issues and unexpected behavior.
Understanding the internals of capability negotiation helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with capability negotiation has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying capability negotiation in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Capability Negotiation is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Capability Negotiation is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Building a custom MCP server is the primary way to extend what Claude can do in your specific domain. The MCP SDK provides a server framework that handles protocol mechanics—JSON-RPC message routing, capability negotiation, error formatting—so you can focus on implementing the actual tool logic your application needs. A custom MCP server exposes its functionality through three primitives: tools (functions Claude can call to take action), resources (data Claude can read), and prompts (reusable conversation templates). You choose which primitives to implement based on your use case. A database integration might expose query execution as a tool, table schemas as resources, and common query patterns as prompts. A CI/CD integration might expose pipeline triggering as a tool and build logs as resources. The server communicates with Claude Desktop, Claude.ai, or your own client over stdio (for local processes), Server-Sent Events (for HTTP-based remote servers), or WebSockets. The implementation overhead is lower than it appears: a minimal working MCP server with one tool requires about 30 lines of Python using the official SDK.
Use the official MCP Python or TypeScript SDK—do not implement the JSON-RPC layer yourself
Start with stdio transport for local development; switch to SSE for remote/multi-user deployments
Validate all tool input arguments before execution—Claude may occasionally pass unexpected types
Return structured JSON from tools so Claude can reliably parse and act on results
Implement graceful error responses using the MCP error format rather than raising raw exceptions
Test your server with the MCP Inspector tool before integrating with Claude Desktop
Version your server in serverInfo.version—clients use this to detect when to re-negotiate capabilities

Tool registration is the process of making Claude aware of what actions your MCP server can perform. When a session starts, the client calls tools/list on the server to receive the complete catalogue of available tools along with their names, descriptions, and JSON Schema input specifications. The quality of tool descriptions is critical: Claude uses them to decide which tool to call and what arguments to supply, entirely based on the description and schema. A vague description like 'does database stuff' forces Claude to guess; a precise description like 'Executes a read-only SQL SELECT statement against the production analytics database and returns results as a JSON array of row objects' gives Claude everything it needs to use the tool correctly without additional prompting. The inputSchema field uses standard JSON Schema to describe the expected arguments, including types, required fields, descriptions, and constraints. If a field has enum values, list them—Claude will use the exact values from your enum rather than guessing. Tool discovery is also dynamic: if your server declares tools.listChanged in its capabilities, it can send a tools/list_changed notification at any time to tell the client its tool catalogue has changed, after which the client should re-fetch the list.
Write descriptions that tell Claude when to use the tool, not just what the tool does
Use additionalProperties: false in input schemas to prevent Claude from supplying undocumented fields
Enumerate every valid enum value—Claude will pick from the list rather than fabricating values
Declare tools.listChanged if your server's tool catalogue can change at runtime (feature flags, user permissions)
Keep tool names lowercase with underscores—they appear in Claude's reasoning and should be self-explanatory
Limit tool catalogues to what is genuinely relevant—50+ tools dilutes Claude's ability to select correctly
Include usage examples in descriptions for tools with non-obvious argument formats
